definitions: (mathematics) In terms of an identity.

definitions: The state or quality of being identical.

definitions: Capable of being distinguished and named.

definitions: In a manner or state that is capable of being distinguished or named.

definitions: A feeling of support, sympathy, understanding, or belonging towards somebody or something.
A particular instance of identifying something.
(strictly) The act of identifying (i.e., which one, which thing).

The state of being identified.

(broadly, often) The combination of identifying (which one, which thing) and validating the identity (proving or confirming it); identifying and authenticating viewed as a unitary concept in cognition.

definitions: simple past and past participle of identify
Something that identifies or uniquely points to something or someone else.
One who identifies as a particular type or role; one who says and believes that they are a certain thing.

Someone who identifies; a person who establishes the identity of someone or something.
A guidebook that helps determine the specific class of an object (such as a mushroom, herb, fish, bird, drug, or mineral), or its individual identity (such as that of a star).

examples: The Identifier personally inspects each horse in each race by verifying the lip tattoo, body color, head and leg markings, scars, and chestnut (night eyes).

definitions: To equate or make the same; to unite or combine into one.

(transitive, biology) To establish the taxonomic classification of an organism.
To establish the identity of someone or something.

definitions: (law enforcement, criminology) A picture of a person, reconstructed from strips showing facial features selected to match witnesses ' descriptions; used by the police to construct a likeness of a person sought for a crime.

definitions: (mathematics) An equation which always holds true regardless of the choice of input variables.
Sameness, identicalness; the quality or fact of (several specified things) being the same.
(algebra, computing) Any function which maps all elements of its domain to themselves.
A name or persona —a mask or appearance one presents to the world—by which one is known.
(Australia, New Zealand) A well-known or famous person.

The difference or character that marks off an individual or collective from the rest of the same kind; selfhood; the sense of who something or someone or oneself is, or the recurring characteristics that enable the recognition of such an individual or group by others or themselves.
(algebra) An element of an algebraic structure which, when applied to another element under an operation in that structure, yields this second element.
examples: The equation (x + y)(x − y) = x 2 − y 2 is an algebraic identity. It is true regardless of the values of x and y.

definitions: A picture or symbol which represents the idea of something without indicating the sequence of sounds used to pronounce it. Examples include digits, traffic signs, and graphic symbols such as @.

definitions: autolatry, or self worship.

definitions: An artistic style (for example, in art, architecture, or music); an instance of such a style.

definitions: Pertaining or conforming to idiom, the natural mode of expression of a language.

definitions: (manner) In an incidental manner.
examples: Incidentally, did you hear anything new from your brother yesterday?

definitions: A desert shrub (Encelia farinosa) producing a resin that emits a fragrant odor when burned.
(neologism, Internet slang, anime and manga) Filter -avoidance spelling of incest.
To destroy by burning.

examples: His mother was incinerated in the Dresden fire-storm. So it goes.

definitions: The act of incinerating, or the state of being incinerated; cremation.

A state of nascency; a quality of incipience.

definitions: To cut in or into with a sharp instrument; to carve; to engrave.
examples: The executioner’s blade is incised with Christ’s crown of thorns, and with the words of a prayer.

definitions: simple past and past participle of incise
(biology) cut, particularly with a V shape

examples: Riyad Mahrez also dragged wide after a De Bruyne incision.

Intelligently analytical and concise. (of a person or mental process)

(anatomy, relational) Of or relating to the incisors.
examples: And her incisive smile accrediting / That treason of false witness in my blush.
An incisive, high voice.
An incisive producer, who expressed vehement disapproval with my pitch upon my first sentence.
the incisive bones, the premaxillaries

definitions: In an incisive manner.
examples: `Yesterday,' said Rupert Hardman, incisively, forensically.

definitions: The quality or state of being incisive; incisivity.
examples: "Merely an amateur," put in Carrados, suavely, but with the incisiveness of a scalpel.

definitions: (anatomy, zootomy) A narrow -edged tooth at the front of the mouth of mammals, between the canines and adapted for cutting; in humans there are four in each jaw.
(anatomy) a notch or indent
(anatomy) A notch or indent.
A cut or incision.
The act of inciting or moving to action.
examples: A notable man, great in yeares, in name, in dignity and in learning, vaunted himselfe unto me, that he was induced to a certaine most important change of his religion, by a strange and fantastical incitation […].

definitions: To call into action.
To instigate a specific incident.
To entreat an act.
examples: The judge was told by the accused that his friends had incited him to commit the crime.

examples: The sheriff was constantly goading me into shooting trespassers, which should surely count as incitement at the very least.

definitions: One who incites.
examples: ‘Someone put those disrespectful imbeciles up to what they did! And I want that inciter in custody else I’ll do what I did in the war, else I’ll show every single one of you why they call me Tuvy!’

definitions: tending to incite
Lack of civilization; a state of rudeness or barbarism.
Any act of rudeness or ill - breeding.
The state of being uncivil; lack of courtesy; rudeness in manner.
examples: Courtezan. How say you now? is not your husband mad? / Adriana. His incivility confirms no less.
Swell curled Waves, high as Jove ’s roof,
Beat on proud Billows, Boreas blow,
Your incivility doth show,

Latona, in her flight from Juno, is churlishly intreated by the Lycian pesants, and denied the publique benefit of water: for which incivility these bawling Clownes are changed into croaking froggs, and confined unto that Lake for ever.

definitions: Something that is inclement.
The quality of being inclement; lack of clemency.
examples: There had been rain, squalls mingled with snow, hailstorms, gusts of wind, but these inclemencies did not last.
with neither house nor clothes to cover them from the inclemencies of the weather
They are built of rough sticks, covered with bulrushes or grass, in such a manner as to completely protect the inhabitants from all the inclemencies of the weather.
The cry of the suffering and dying rings in our ears, as they are dragged from their beds, to be exposed to the inclemencies of the ice-covered sea in an open boat.

definitions: Stormy, of rough weather; not clement.
examples: inclement weather

By some paradoxical evolution rancour and intolerance have been established in the vanguard of primitive Christianity. Mrs. Spoker, in common with many of the stricter disciples of righteousness, was as inclement in demeanour as she was cadaverous in aspect.

He lived in the world, as the last of the Grisly Bears lived in settled Missouri. And as when Spring and Summer had departed, that wild Logan of the woods, burying himself in the hollow of a tree, lived out the winter there, sucking his own paws; so, in his inclement, howling old age, Ahab’s soul, shut up in the caved trunk of his body, there fed upon the sullen paws of its gloom!

definitions: The state of being inclement
A tendency.
(geometry) The angle of intersection of a reference plane.
A slant or slope.
A physical tilt or bend.
examples: The inclination of his head increased and he awoke with a start.
His inclination to drink escalated to alcoholism.
Artillery must take account of a weapon's precise inclination.
The road up to the house had a steep inclination.
you make will be a Discovery of your Inclinations

definitions: A slope.
A portal of a subway tunnel.
examples: To reach the building, we had to climb a steep incline.
the Pleasant Street Incline

definitions: (chiefly intransitive, chiefly passive voice) To tend to do or believe something, or move or be moved in a certain direction, away from a point of view, attitude, etc.
To slope.
To bend or move (something) out of a given plane or direction, often the horizontal or vertical.
examples: Over the centuries the wind made the walls of the farmhouse incline.
He had to incline his body against the gusts to avoid being blown down in the storm.
I'm inclined to give up smoking after hearing of the risks to my health.
He inclines to believe anything he reads in the newspapers.
The people following the coffin inclined their heads in grief.

definitions: simple past and past participle of incline
Having a tendency, preference, likelihood, or disposition.
At an angle to the horizontal; slanted or sloped.
examples: She's inclined to lose her temper.
The take-off ramp was inclined at 20 degrees.
I am inclined to believe you.

definitions: inclination
examples: The removal of the monarch and his court to the Brazils had tended to make the nobles less loyal in their inclinings […]

Monsieur Rigaud sometimes stopped, as if he were going to put his case in a new light, or make some irate remonstrance; but Signor Cavalletto continuing to go slowly to and fro at a grotesque kind of jog-trot pace with his eyes turned downward, nothing came of these inclinings.

definitions: present participle and gerund of incline
An instrument that displays the angle of an aircraft relative to the horizon.
A surveying instrument that measures angles of inclination or elevation; a clinometer.
An instrument that measures magnetic dip; a dip circle.
examples: For the ground, that means using three primary types of equipment: ■ Extensometers to look for ground heave - the upwards movement of soil, which can occur at any stage. It is essentially the opposite of erosion and is often associated with clay-type soils which swell when wet. ■ Inclinometers, which measure slope movements. ■ Piezometers to measure water levels.

definitions: (programming) A piece of source code or other content that is dynamically retrieved for inclusion in another item.
examples: In the previous lesson, you learned how to use server-side includes, which enable you to easily include snippets of web pages within other web pages.

definitions: (programming) To use a directive that allows the use of source code from another file.
To bring into a group, class, set, or total as a (new) part or member.
To consider as part of something; to comprehend.
examples: I could have here willingly ranged, but these straits wherein I am included will not permit.
I was included in the invitation to the family gathering.
The vacation package includes car rental.
You have to include the strings library to use this function.
Does this volume of Shakespeare include his sonnets?

definitions: simple past and past participle of include
Provided as an accompaniment.
(botany) Enclosed, not protruding, e.g. stamens within the corolla.
examples: Read the included leaflet for instructions.

definitions: (cytology) A nuclear or cytoplasmic aggregate of stainable substances.
An addition or annex to a group, set, or total.
(countable, mineralogy) Any material that is trapped inside a mineral during its formation, as a defect in a precious stone.
Anything foreign that is included in a material.
(mathematics) A mapping where the domain is a subset of the image.
The act of including, i.e. adding or annexing, (something) to a group, set, or total.
(histology) An object completely inside a tissue, such as epidermal inclusion cyst, a cyst in the epidermis.
examples: The poem was a new inclusion in the textbook.
The inclusion of the poem added value to the course.
The fewer inclusions a diamond has, the better is its clarity and value. Often the inclusions can be cut out of a diamond in the rough.

definitions: Including (almost) everything within its scope.
Including or accepting those belonging to a particular group.
Including the extremes as well as the area between.
(linguistics) Of, or relating to the first-person plural pronoun when including the person being addressed.
examples: The pronoun in "If you want, we could go back to my place for coffee" is an inclusive "we".
an inclusive list of data formats
trans- inclusive feminism
numbers 1 to 10 inclusive

definitions: One unknown or in disguise, or under an assumed character or name.
The assumption of disguise or of a feigned character; the state of being in disguise or not recognized.
examples: It contained a letter from the King himself, craving hospitality for a few days, as his mother was about to visit England, and to take up with Lord Avonleigh her residence at the Castle. A slight incognito would be preserved, and as little form and ceremony expected as was possible.

definitions: Without being known; in an assumed character, or under an assumed title; in disguise.
examples: THE Scepticks think 'twas long ago, / Since Gods came down Incognito; / To ſee who were their Friends or Foes, / And how our Actions fell or roſe.

definitions: Without revealing one's identity.
examples: The prince royal of Prussia came thither incognito. The spelling has been modernized.

Yet the matter was so delicate that I could not confide it to an agent without putting myself in his power. I have come incognito from Prague for the purpose of consulting you."

definitions: Not cognizable; incapable of being recognised.
examples: All this time the incognizable nouveau was smoking slowly and calmly, and looking at nothing at all with his black buttonlike eyes.

definitions: Failure to cognize, apprehend, or notice.
examples: Now, this incognizance may be explained on three possible hypotheses.

definitions: Lacking knowledge; unaware (of)

The quality of being incoherent.
examples: This was strangely heightened at times by the ragged Elijah’s diabolical incoherences uninvitedly recurring to me, with a subtle energy I could not have before conceived of.

[…] Incoherences in Matter and Suppositions, without Proofs put handsomly together in good Words and a plausible Stile, are apt to pass for strong Reason and good Sense, till they come to be look’d into with Attention.

definitions: A thing which is incoherent.

But that vvhich vvould beſt of all juſtifie me, and the ſeeming incoherencies of ſome parts of my Diſcourse, vvould be the noble Authors Piece it ſelf, becauſe of the Antitheſis, and the forms of his Applications.

definitions: Not coherent.

definitions: (business, commerce) Money coming in to a fund, account, or policy.

more abundant incomes of light and strength from God

definitions: Succeeding to an office or other position.

definitions: Out of proportion (in size, degree or extent) with something else.

definitions: To make (someone) uncomfortable; to discomfort, to disturb, to trouble.

definitions: Discomforting, inconvenient, or disagreeable.

examples: Tellson's Bank by Temple Bar was an old-fashioned place, even in the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty. It was very small, very dark, very ugly, very incommodious.

definitions: In a manner in which communication with outsiders is not possible, for either voluntary or involuntary reasons, especially due to confinement or reclusiveness.
examples: The Starz seem most comfortable when they're in the studio, where they can work incommunicado.

definitions: Uncommunicative.
examples: The author tries hard to breathe life into his subject, but it seems there was little vitality left in the octogenarian Djuna. At her best she was vituperative and incommunicative. She says to Mr. Field that he has come too late to write a book about her.

definitions: The quality or state of being incommutable.
Not commutable
Something beyond compare; a thing with which there is no comparison.
(literally, not comparable) Not comparable.
(figuratively, comparable) So much better than another as to be beyond comparison.
examples: I know of nothing in all drama more incomparable from the point of view of art, nothing more suggestive in its subtlety of observation, than Shakespeare's drawing of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern.

definitions: In an incomparable manner.

[…] if in this extatick State of Dreaming (during which the Astral Impulses are incomparably stronger than in common Dreams, or in the ordinary Course of Life) that prophetick Energy more efficaciously exerts itself […]

definitions: The quality or state of being incompatible; inconsistency; irreconcilability.

(philosophy) A consequent of a contrary.

Incompatibles are consequents of contraries. For example, sleeping and waking are contraries, and snoring is associated with sleepers. So snoring and waking are incompatibles.

definitions: (chemistry) Incapable of being together without mutual reaction or decomposition, as certain medicines.

examples: My phone is incompatible with the latest version of this app.

definitions: In an incompatible manner.
An inability to perform; a lack of competence; ineptitude.

definitions: The condition of being incompetent.
A person who is incompetent.

definitions: Unable to make rational decisions; insane or otherwise cognitively impaired.
(medicine, of the cervix) Opening too early during pregnancy, resulting in miscarriage or premature birth.

(geology) Not resistant to deformation or flow.

definitions: In an incompetent manner.
examples: He’s like more successful but equally morally abhorrent right-wing undercover operative James O’Keefe’s non-union Mexican equivalent, a dunce whose one move involves incompetently attempting to frame figures of seemingly unimpeachable moral integrity, like Robert Mueller or good old Mayor Pete, as deviant sex criminals like Wohl’s God Donald Trump by unsuccessfully trying to get people to come forward with bogus sexual assault allegations.

definitions: A designation of being incomplete.

definitions: Not complete; not finished.

definitions: To an incomplete degree.
In an incomplete manner.
The state or condition of being not complete.

definitions: The condition of being incomprehensible.
Something that cannot be understood.
examples: That left Bradly as bemused as ever, for it posed another incomprehensibility: Why had Podson walked here?

definitions: Anything that is beyond understanding.
(theology or literary) Which cannot be contained; boundless, infinite.

definitions: Lack of comprehension or understanding; inability to understand.
examples: Simon broke off and turned to Piggy who was looking at him with an expression of derisive incomprehension.

definitions: Not comprehensive; shallow, incomplete.
Uncomprehending.
examples: So she for those first few moments stood incomprehensive and stared with empty eyes.

definitions: (physics) The bulk modulus or modulus of incompressibility, measured in either electron volts or dynes per square centimeter.

examples: Water has a high degree of incompressibility.

definitions: Not compressible.
(calculus, of a vector field) Having a divergence equal to zero.
examples: As water [trapped in the cylinders] is incompressible, a momentary leap in [steam] pressure is liable to occur in starting; […].

definitions: Not computable; that cannot be computed, calculated or estimated.
examples: When ordinary coaching stock is available, the numbers massed into each compartment are simply incomputable from the outside.

definitions: The quality of being inconceivable.
Something inconceivable.
Unable to be conceived or imagined; unbelievable.
examples: While the world altered dramatically during the course of her reign, the monarchy did too, though rather more imperceptibly: the walkabouts that increasingly characterised royal appearances, the pop concerts at Buckingham Palace, the throwing open of the royal palaces to visitors – even the paying of income tax, and royal podcasts – would have been inconceivable as innovations at the time Elizabeth came to the throne.

definitions: The quality of being inconceivable.

definitions: In an inconceivable manner, in a way not believable or understandable.

definitions: In an inconclusive manner.

definitions: illogical; not following from the premises

definitions: Anything inedible; that which is not a foodstuff.

The co-existent Flame / Knew the Destroyer; it encircled him, / Roll'd up his robe, and gathered round his head, / Condensing to intenser splendour there, / His crown of glory, and his light of life, / Hovered the irradiate wreath.

definitions: An act of irradiating, or state of being irradiated.
(mathematics) A real number that can not be expressed as the quotient of two integers, an irrational number.
examples: The square root of 2, which was the first irrational to be discovered, was known to the early Pythagoreans, and ingenious methods of approximating to its value were discovered.

definitions: (mathematics, arithmetic, number theory, not comparable) Of a real number, that cannot be written as the ratio of two integers.
Not rational; unfounded, nonsensical or wrong-headed.
examples: The number π is irrational.
an irrational decision

definitions: The quality or state of being irrational.
Something that is irrational; the result of an illogical or absurd thought or action.
examples: Man introduces reason into the world of Nature, destroying irrational struggle and waste. But this activity is outside of self, distant, only reflected. Man sees this irrationality only with his intellect.

definitions: In an irrational manner; without reason; in a manner contrary to reason
examples: Some people that irrationally hate the military as a whole *might* just qualify as well.

Financial behaviourists state that market valuation breaks away from economic rationales of fundamental data if three conditions are met: firstly, investors behave irrationally in great numbers; secondly, irrational behaviour is not random but systematic; thirdly, rational investors' abilities are constrained in that arbitrage position can correct biases from intrinsic value.

“I’m instructing you and my client to avoid all interrogation. Roosevelt is on strong narcotics for pain and could answer irrationally. I don’t want him held accountable for something he may say while under the adverse effects of narcotics.’ [’] I said lawyerly.

definitions: Unredeemable.
Incapable of being reclaimed; not reclaimable.
examples: an irreclaimable criminal
irreclaimable land

definitions: Something that cannot be reconciled.
Incompatible, discrepant, contradictory.
Unable to be reconciled; opposed; uncompromising.
examples: I amused myself by thinking that in his choice of books he showed pleasantly the irreconcilable sides of his fantastic nature.

definitions: Not recoverable; incapable of being recovered

an irrecoverable disease; irrecoverable danger

definitions: (finance) A financial instrument that cannot be freely redeemed.

definitions: Any region whose inhabitants are ethnically related to those of another state

examples: Ukraine became a major concern to [Stalin’s] “fortress builders” on the eve of World War II. The defensive bulwark seemed to have developed into a large crack – the threat of Ukrainian irredentism.

definitions: Of or relating to irredentists or their policies.
examples: In 1885, a Bulgarian irredentist movement seized control of neighbouring Ottoman-ruled Eastern Roumelia and announced the creation of a Greater Bulgaria.

definitions: (mathematics) Such a polynomial
Not able to be reduced or lessened.
(mathematics, of a polynomial) Unable to be factorized into polynomials of lower degree, as (x + 1).
(number theory, of an integer) Unable to be factored into smaller integers; prime.
(number theory, of a fraction) Whose numerator and denominator share no common factor greater than 1.
examples: With each reduction in the number of railways, there must come, eventually, a decline in interest, if only through reduction in variety; and when it comes to one nationalised railway only we have reached the irreducible minimum.

definitions: One who does not regularly attend a venue.

definitions: (geometry, of a polygon) Not regular; having sides that are not equal or angles that are not equal.

definitions: An instance of being irregular.
An object or event that is not regular or ordinary.

definitions: In an irregular manner; without rule, method, or order.

definitions: Not relevant, as:

definitions: The state or quality of being irreligious; ungodliness.

definitions: Such that it cannot be repaired.

(video games) The system of items available to players and their design and qualities.
examples: His itemization of the inventory did seem accurate, however it omitted several crucial entries.

definitions: To state in items, or by particulars
examples: to itemize the cost of a railroad

definitions: (mathematics) The image of a certain value under such a function.
(mathematics) An n -fold self-composition of a function.
examples: f 2 (x 0) is the second iterate of x 0 under f.
An important example of such a semigroup in infinite dimensional Hilbert space is the weak operator closed monothetic semigroup generated by a linear operator with equibounded iterates.

definitions: (computing) To perform or repeat an action on each item in a set.
(design, ambitransitive) To repeat an activity, making incremental changes each time.
(computing, mathematics) To perform or repeat an action on the results of each such prior action.
examples: The max() function iterates through the data to find the highest value.
In mathematics, an iterated function is a function which is composed with itself, possibly ad infinitum, in a process called iteration.
to iterate advice

For NASA and most private aerospace companies, a single crash is a setback that can take years to recover from. SpaceX works more like a Silicon Valley startup, where the goal is to fail quickly and iterate.

definitions: Said or done again; repeated.
(as a participle) Iterated.
examples: When our faith is otherwise well enough known, there needs no iterate confession, saith Baldwin, which if, as he saith, it be vain boasting rather than a Christian vertue in us to offer it, it can be no lesse than needlesse, imperious usurpation in them to call as to it.

definitions: (computing) The use of repetition in a computer program, especially in the form of a loop.
A variation or version.
A recital or a second performance; a repetition.
(computing) A single repetition of the code within such a repetitive process.
examples: The architect drafted several iterations of the floorplan before deciding on his final design.
The code calculates the appropriate value at each iteration.

definitions: (grammar) A verb showing the iterative aspect.
(grammar) Expressive of an action that is repeated with frequency.
Of a procedure that involves repetition of steps (iteration) to achieve the desired outcome; in computing this may involve a mechanism such as a loop.
(Ireland) A member of the Travelling Community, whether settled or not.
One who travels from place to place.
Habitually travelling from place to place. [from 1560s]
examples: an itinerant preacher or peddler

definitions: An account or record of a journey.
A route or proposed route of a journey.
A guidebook for travellers.
A written schedule of activities for a vacation or road trip.
examples: The length of the Wengernalp Railway from Lauterbrunnen over the Kleine Scheidegg to Grindelwald or vice versa, Riggenbach rack-and-pinion operated throughout, should be a "must" in the itinerary of every Oberland tourist; [...].

definitions: Itinerant; travelling from place to place; done on a journey.
examples: It were rather an itinerary circuit of justice than a progress.

definitions: To travel from place to place, especially to preach or lecture.
(formal) A travelling around.
examples: In company with William I made several interesting itinerations on the hills during the last cold season, an account of which has been sent home to the Board; also a tour of upwards of 350 miles on the plains […]

definitions: (pharmacology) A particular triazole antifungal agent (trademark Sporanox) that may be given orally or intravenously especially to treat blastomycosis and histoplasmosis.
Overgrown with ivy or another climbing plant.
examples: But he was broodingly unable to see even the most ivied tower as anything but a pile of stones till, inexplicably, the miracle of recovered hope and courage transformed him.
Even a place as innocent and unsqualid as the ivied Wesleyan University in Connecticut provokes contempt […]
[…] great glossy-leaved mango trees ivied with pepper vines […]
[…] Lancelot Carnaby stopped from his rash venture into the water, and drew himself back into an ivied bush, which served as the finial of the little garden-hedge.

definitions: (slang, ethnic slur) A white person.
The hard white form of dentin which forms the tusks of elephants, walruses and other animals.
(informal) A die (object bearing numbers, thrown in games of chance).
(collective, singular or in plural) The teeth.
A creamy white color, the color of ivory.
Something made from or resembling ivory.
(collective, singular or in plural) The keys of a piano; or, the white keys, as opposed to the black keys (ebonies).
examples: Tony had gone into the storage room and gotten two more standing lamps, one placed near the ebonies and ivories.
to tickle the ivories
Ye forgot some o' yer ivories, didn't ye, on th' grass?
The triumphant Englishman did not give himself the trouble even to shake the dice; and, so sure was he of winning, that he threw the ivory on the table without looking.
“Leave the ivory!” he cried. “Leave the ivory! Dead men have no use for ivory!” Some of the Manyuema started to lay down their loads, but this was altogether too much for the avaricious Arabs.

definitions: Resembling or having the colour of ivory.
Made of ivory.
examples: The walls and ceiling of this drawing-room in Montague Square are painted ivory.
Men that I knew around Wapatomac didn't wear high, shiny plug hats, nor yeller spring overcoats, nor carry canes with ivory heads as big as a catboat's anchor, as you might say.

definitions: An ivory-billed woodpecker (Campephilus principalis).
Pronunciation spelling of every.
(by extension) Any similar plant of any genus.
Any of several woody, climbing, or trailing evergreen plants of the genus Hedera.
examples: Hidden timber elements, lintels and roof tiles have also been replaced, in addition to gaining a new paint job and guttering drainage, and the removal of ivy that was damaging the exterior.

definitions: Any of the genus Ixia of cormous plants native to South Africa.
plural of ixode
(zoology) Any of various ticks of the family Ixodidae.
(African-American Vernacular) third-person singular simple present indicative of be
(Geordie) Eye dialect spelling of us (“ me ”).
(Scotland, and archaically in England and Ireland) The letter Z; zed, zee.
examples: ... [Reading.] Dear Sir,—ay, that's that. Then there's an M, and a T, and an S, but whether the next be an izzard, or an R, confound me, I cannot tell.
